title: Survivorship Thoughts
body:
Hi all, I'm 2.5 years into survivorship and just wanted to put some thoughts out there that might help others, and also provide a space to share experiences or ask questions.

DX: Stage 1b Triple positive IDC

TX: THCP 6 rounds, an additional 12 rounds Herceptin (a full 12 months on it). Nipple sparing BMX with tissue expanders to silicone implants. On Anastrozole for 5-10 years.

1. It took more than a full year after stopping Herceptin before I got my stamina and energy back. I was not super active prior to treatment, and was not active at all during treatment. Before treatment I could walk a couple miles and be ok. Once my stamina started to come back, it took another six months to start to feel strong enough to do a couple of miles and not be exhausted or winded. Be kind to yourself as you recover.

2. Survivorship was hard for the first two years. I didn't stop grieving my prior life and my cancer until about six months ago. I think about it daily still, and definitely have my moments of being angry about it, but it isn't all-consuming like it was before.

3. Side effects are real. Anastrozole wreaked havoc on my body. Hot flashes, night sweats, constant joint pain, fatigue in the beginning. I manage the joint pain by taking Cymbalta (bonus is it treats my depression and anxiety), joint health supplement, tart cherry juice pill, and Claritin daily. It sucks but it works for me. I also use the heating pad or soak in a hot tub on bad days. I manage the hot flashes by taking Veozah. It literally saved me because I would not have continued the Anastrozole if it didn't work.

4. Hair loss isn't the same for everyone. I had fine, thin hair before treatment so I wasn't too heartbroken when I lost my hair. The hardest part was losing my eyebrows and eyelashes. Two and a half years later, my brows are non-existent, my lashes are thin, and my hair has thin spots. I attribute it to the Anastrozole, but I also think I have some scarring from treatment so I may never have a full head of hair, full lashes, or eyebrows again. This is the part of survivorship that I get angry about. I have a haircut that suits me and that's fine, but I didn't choose it. I can't have the haircut I want because my hair is just too thin for it to look good. 

5. Find your support groups, even after treatment ends. [Breastcancer.org](http://Breastcancer.org) support groups saved me. There are women who have been part of the groups for years after treatment because it's a true community. I thought I would stop attending once I felt ok about my cancer experience and then realized I missed the women. We give life updates and sometimes that has to do with appointments, but a lot of times it's just what's going on in the day-to-day. There are women all over the world that attend and it's a beautiful community. They also have online forums you can keep up with if you would rather not be on a Zoom call.

6. Don't blame yourself for your cancer. If a person lives long enough, they will eventually get cancer. Some people just get it younger than others, and other people die before they have the chance to get it.

7. Give yourself grace in how you cope. My coping after treatment (and still to this day to manage other life events) is drinking. I've reduced how much I consume daily, but for awhile I was having 6-8 drinks a day to escape. Time and therapy have helped me tremendously. I still drink daily but much less than I was. We are all a work in progress.
